query-multiple-columns.label: Query multiple columns
query-multiple-columns.summary: Learn how to select multiple columns from a table using SQL's SELECT statement. Practice writing queries that retrieve more than one column and understand how the result set changes.

intro-multicolumn-select.title: Selecting Multiple Columns
intro-multicolumn-select.bodyMarkdown:
In SQL, you can retrieve more than one column from a table by listing the column names separated by commas in your SELECT statement. For example, if you have a table called `students` with columns `id`, `name`, and `grade`, you can write:

```sql
SELECT name, grade FROM students;
```

This query returns both the `name` and `grade` columns for every student in the table.

why-multicolumn.title: Why Select Multiple Columns?
why-multicolumn.bodyMarkdown: Selecting multiple columns is useful when you need more information than just a single attribute. For instance, viewing both the product name and price helps you compare items. Listing only the columns you need makes your queries faster and results easier to read.

fillblank-multicolumn-basic.title: Fill in the blank: Selecting two columns
fillblank-multicolumn-basic.prompt:
Suppose you want to see both the `name` and `grade` of every student from the `students` table. Complete the query:

SELECT ____ FROM students;
fillblank-multicolumn-basic.hint: List both columns separated by a comma.

help.concept: To select multiple columns, write their names separated by commas after SELECT.
help.hint_1: You need both column names, in the order asked.
help.hint_2: Write 'the missing term' after SELECT to get both columns.

fillblank-multicolumn-order.title: Fill in the blank: Column order matters
fillblank-multicolumn-order.prompt: If you write `SELECT grade, name FROM students;`, which column will appear first in the results?
fillblank-multicolumn-order.hint: The order in SELECT determines the order in the result.

help.concept: The order of columns in the SELECT clause matches the order in the result set.
help.hint_1: Look at which column is listed first after SELECT.
help.hint_2: The first column after SELECT is the first in the output.

fillblank-multicolumn-order.template: The first column in the results will be: ____

singlechoice-select-star.title: Single choice: What does SELECT * do?
singlechoice-select-star.prompt: What does the query `SELECT * FROM students;` return?

help.concept: SELECT * returns every column in the table for each row.
help.hint_2: SELECT * gives you every column, not just a few.

options.a: All columns from the students table
options.b: Only the first column from the students table
options.d: No columns, just the row count

multichoice-why-not-select-star.title: Multi choice: Why avoid SELECT * in real applications?
multichoice-why-not-select-star.prompt: Why is it often better to list specific columns instead of using SELECT *? (Choose all that apply.)
multichoice-why-not-select-star.hint: Think about performance, code safety, and clarity.

help.concept: Listing columns improves performance, stability, and readability.
help.hint_1: SELECT * can return unnecessary data and cause issues if the table changes.
help.hint_2: Specifying columns is safer and clearer for everyone.

options.a: It makes queries faster by returning only needed data
options.b: It avoids breaking your code if the table changes
options.c: It makes your intent clearer to others

codeinput-multicolumn-query.title: Code input: Write a multi-column SELECT
codeinput-multicolumn-query.prompt: Write a SQL query to return the `name` and `grade` columns from the `students` table.
codeinput-multicolumn-query.hint: Focus on the concept being tested.

help.concept: Think about the role or idea being tested rather than repeating answer wording.
help.hint_1: Eliminate choices or interpretations that do not match the task.
help.hint_2: Choose the concept that best fits what the exercise is asking you to do.
